title: Devilled Mackerel
subtitle: Mackerel fillets scored, rubbed with mustard and cayenne, grilled until charred and crisp
excerpt: Devilled mackerel uses the oily richness of the fish as a counterpoint to the sharp, hot devilled marinade. Score the skin deeply, rub in the mustard and spice paste, and grill under a very hot grill until the skin blisters and chars. The whole thing takes under 15 minutes.
sourceNotes: British tradition. The devilled preparation for oily fish, particularly mackerel, is documented in British cookbooks from the 18th century and was a standard item on the Victorian breakfast sideboard alongside devilled kidneys. The sharp mustard and spice preparation cuts the richness of the fish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score and rub the mackerel up to 2 hours ahead. Keep refrigerated and grill just before serving.
recipe.temperatureNote: Grilled under a very hot overhead grill; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: To make shallow cuts in the surface of meat or fish with a sharp knife. Scoring fish allows marinades to penetrate, prevents the skin from curling under heat, and creates more surface area for browning and charring. Cuts should go through the skin and just into the flesh.

content.text: Devilled mackerel is a fast, direct dish: score the skin, rub in the devilled paste, grill hard for 5 to 6 minutes per side. The mackerel's fat is the counterpoint to the mustard's sharpness and the cayenne's heat; oily fish and pungent seasoning are a natural pairing. The skin should blister and char; that char is part of the flavour.

items.prepNote: half for the rub, half in wedges to serve

content.text: Heat the grill to its highest setting. Line a baking tray with foil and lightly oil it.
each {{mackerel-fillets}} mackerel fillet 3 to 4 times through the skin with a sharp knife, cutting at an angle about 1 cm deep.

Mix the Dijon mustard, mustard powder, cayenne, Worcestershire sauce, and a squeeze of lemon juice into a paste. Rub this devilled paste over the scored side and into the cuts. Season with salt and pepper.

Place the fillets skin-side up on the prepared tray. Grill close to the element for 5 to 6 minutes until the skin blisters, chars in patches, and the flesh is cooked through. There is no need to turn the fillets. Rest for 2 minutes and serve with lemon wedges.

items.symptom: Skin does not blister or char
items.cause: Grill not hot enough or tray placed too far from the element.
items.fix: The grill must be at maximum heat. Place the tray as close to the element as possible; the skin should be blistering within 2 to 3 minutes.

items.cause: Scores too shallow or fillets not dried before rubbing.
items.fix: Pat the mackerel dry before rubbing. Score deeply enough that the cuts hold the paste. A slightly thicker paste, with less lemon juice, also adheres better.

items.symptom: Fish is undercooked at the centre
items.cause: Grill element was too far away, or the fillet was very thick.
items.fix: Mackerel fillets are usually thin enough to cook through in 5 to 6 minutes. If the flesh is not opaque at the thickest point after 6 minutes, continue under the grill for 1 to 2 more minutes.

content.text: Devilled mackerel belongs to the same family as devilled kidneys and devilled eggs: all use the classic British devilled seasoning of mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce. The combination appears across British cooking from the 18th century, applied most often to offal, oily fish, and leftover meat from the previous day's roast. Mackerel is the best fish for this treatment because its fat can absorb the sharp, hot paste without being overwhelmed by it. Leaner white fish would dry out and would not carry the seasoning in the same way.
